skill.name: pci-compliance
skill.description: Apply PCI DSS guidance for payment systems, cardholder data handling, tokenization, encryption, access control, audit logging, and compliance readiness.
skill.summary: Guides PCI DSS planning and secure payment data handling.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown code fences, PCI terminology, and instructional examples. One real issue remains: a payment processor example shows a hardcoded secret key placeholder, which is unsafe guidance for a PCI skill and should be corrected before broad publication.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Payment Processor Secret in Example
medium_findings.description: The Stripe example assigns a secret key placeholder directly in code. This is not evidence of credential theft or malicious behavior, but it teaches a risky pattern for payment systems because real secret keys should come from a secret manager or protected environment configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line directly assigns a Stripe-style secret key placeholder in an example. Confidence is high for unsafe guidance, but there is no evidence that the skill contains a real secret or exfiltrates data.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misidentified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: The static command-execution findings are markdown code fences or a markdown file reference, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. No executable shell command, subprocess call, or user-controlled command string was found at these cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visible markdown fences or inline markdown references in SKILL.md. They do not invoke a shell, run Ruby backticks, or define executable skill scripts.

low_findings.title: Encryption Key Handling Examples Misclassified as Sensitive Files
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file findings point to variables and AES-GCM object creation inside explanatory encryption examples. They do not reference certificate files, private key files, or filesystem reads of key material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is an AES-GCM educational example. The lines handle in-memory encryption variables and provide no evidence of certificate or private key file access.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Detection Is a SameSite Cookie False Positive
low_findings.description: The critical Windows SAM database finding is caused by a SameSite cookie configuration line. It does not access Windows credential databases or operating system security files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line sets SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE to Strict. This is unrelated to Windows SAM and has no credential-database access behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Misclassified PCI Text
low_findings.description: The cited weak-cryptography locations are the skill description, a payment description string, and a list item warning against storing CVV. No weak cryptographic primitive such as DES or MD5 is used at these cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines found descriptive PCI text, not weak cryptographic algorithms. The skill elsewhere recommends AES-256-GCM, which is not the flagged issue.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Detections Are Payment Workflow Text
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance findings map to comments about sending payment tokens, storing payment method identifiers, and a reference to access-control documentation. No scanner, enumeration command, or network probing logic appears at these cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text describes payment token handling and documentation references. There is no evidence of host discovery, port scanning, account enumeration, or network reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Secret Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation contains a direct assignment of a Stripe-style secret key placeholder. This should be changed to show environment-backed or secret-manager-backed configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded key assignment is explicit at the cited line. It is a documentation risk rather than a confirmed runtime vulnerability.

content.value_statement: Payment systems must protect cardholder data and reduce compliance scope. This skill guides PCI DSS controls, tokenization, encryption, access control, audit logging, and assessment preparation.
actual_capabilities: Explains the 12 PCI DSS control areas in practical terms.
Recommends data minimization rules for PAN, CVV, track data, and PIN data.
Shows tokenization patterns for hosted payment and processor-token workflows.
Outlines encryption, TLS, access control, and audit logging controls.
Helps choose SAQ scope based on how card data enters the system.
Provides checklist-style guidance for compliance readiness reviews.
limitations: It does not replace a Qualified Security Assessor or legal advice.
It does not certify that a system is PCI DSS compliant.
Some referenced support files are not included in the package.
One example should be updated to avoid showing hardcoded secret keys.

use_cases.title: Scope a Hosted Checkout Integration
use_cases.description: Map a hosted checkout design to likely PCI responsibilities and identify ways to keep card data out of local systems.

use_cases.title: Review Payment Data Storage
use_cases.description: Check whether a payment application stores prohibited data and define controls for encrypted PAN storage.

use_cases.title: Prepare for an Internal PCI Review
use_cases.description: Create a practical checklist for firewall, access control, logging, vulnerability management, and policy evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Identify PCI Scope
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner assessment of a payment flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PCI compliance skill to review this payment flow. Identify which systems touch cardholder data and suggest ways to reduce PCI scope.

prompt_templates.title: Check Stored Data Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PCI compliance skill to review these payment database fields. Flag prohibited storage and recommend safe replacements.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tokenized Payments
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PCI compliance skill to design a tokenized payment flow for recurring billing. Include client-side, server-side, and storage responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Build a PCI Evidence Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PCI compliance skill to create an evidence plan for PCI DSS review. Cover access control, logging, encryption, vulnerability management, and policy artifacts.

output_examples.input: Review our checkout flow where the browser posts card data to our API before calling a processor.
output_examples.output: The API is in PCI scope because it receives card data. Move card capture to a hosted page or processor JavaScript tokenization flow. Store only processor tokens and customer references.

output_examples.input: Check whether we can store card number, expiration date, CVV, and cardholder name.
output_examples.output: Do not store CVV. Store PAN only if encrypted and justified. Expiration date and cardholder name may be stored with appropriate controls and access restrictions.

output_examples.input: Create a readiness checklist for a small e-commerce team using hosted checkout.
output_examples.output: Confirm hosted checkout boundaries, document payment data flows, verify TLS settings, restrict administrative access, retain audit logs, review vendor attestations, and prepare SAQ evidence.

best_practices: Prefer hosted payment pages or processor tokenization to keep card data out of your systems.
Never store CVV, full track data, PIN, or PIN block values after authorization.
Use secret management, access reviews, audit logging, and evidence collection from the start.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder secret keys into application source code.
Do not treat tokenization as a full substitute for access control and logging.
Do not assume SAQ scope without documenting every system that can affect payment data.

faq.question: Does this skill make my organization PCI compliant?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and review prompts, but compliance validation requires formal assessment and evidence.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What payment data should never be stored?
faq.answer: Do not store CVV, full magnetic stripe data, PIN, or PIN block data after authorization.

faq.question: What is the safest way to reduce PCI scope?
faq.answer: Use hosted checkout or processor-side tokenization so your systems do not receive raw card data.

faq.question: Does it include runnable audit scripts?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the package. The file structure only includes SKILL.md.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing this skill?
faq.answer: Update the payment processor example so secret keys come from secure configuration, not hardcoded source text.
